title: Masking in GPT-Style Models
concept: Causal masking is the defining feature of decoder-only (GPT-style) models, turning them into autoregressive generators. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Training at Scale.'

exercises.prompt: What is the main task that GPT-style models are designed to do?

options.text: Predicting the very next word in a sequence
Filling in missing words in the middle of a paragraph
Sorting emails into spam or not spam

exercises.explain: GPT-style models are autoregressive generators, meaning they generate text word-by-word, using their own previous outputs as the context for the next step.

cards.title: No Peeking!
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how ChatGPT predicts the very next word so quickly? It's not magic—it's because the model is trained with a special set of rules that prevents it from cheating by looking ahead!

cards.title: What is Causal Masking?
cards.body: Causal masking is a technique used in GPT-style (decoder-only) models. It acts like a blindfold that blocks the model from looking at words that come after the current word. This ensures the model only uses past information to predict the next word.

cards.title: The Reading Bookmark
cards.body: Imagine writing a mystery novel but you aren't allowed to look at the next pages. You can only read what you have written so far to decide what word comes next. A causal mask is like a bookmark that blocks your eyes from seeing the future pages of the book, keeping the mystery intact!

cards.title: Masking in Action
cards.body: If the model is training on the sentence 'The cat sat on the mat', when it is predicting the word 'sat', the causal mask completely hides 'on the mat'. The model only sees 'The cat' and must guess what comes next.

cards.title: The Whole Sentence Myth
cards.body: People often think GPT models look at the entire sentence all at once during training, just like Google Translate does. In reality, GPT is strictly forbidden from looking ahead! It must learn to predict step-by-step, word-by-word.

cards.title: Bidirectional vs. Causal
cards.body: Compare how a bidirectional model (like BERT) looks at text versus how a causal model (like GPT) looks at text.

left.text: Can see both past and future words at the same time to understand context.

right.text: Uses a mask to block future words, looking only at the past to generate the next word.

exercises.prompt: What is the main job of a causal mask in a GPT-style model?

options.text: It translates words into different languages.
It stops the model from looking at future words.
It deletes random words to make the training harder.
It speeds up the computer hardware.

exercises.explain: Causal masking blocks the future, forcing the model to rely only on the past words. This matches how humans write text from left to right.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Without causal masking, a GPT-style model would cheat during training by looking at the words it is supposed to predict.
exercises.explain: If the model could see future words, it would just copy them instead of learning how to predict them. Hiding the future is essential for training generators.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the explanation of causal masking.
exercises.sentence: Causal masking works by hiding all of the _____ words from the model during training.
exercises.explain: By hiding future words, the model is forced to learn how to generate text step-by-step using only what has been written so far.

exercises.prompt: Two of these statements about causal masking are correct. Spot the incorrect AI-generated statement!

options.text: A decoder-only model uses a mask to hide future words during training.
Without masking, the model would cheat by looking at the answer key.
Causal masking hides the past words so the model only focuses on the future.

exercises.explain: Option C is generated by an AI that doesn't understand causal masking. Causal masking does not hide past words; it hides future words so the model can learn to predict what comes next.

exercises.prompt: Which key mechanism prevents GPT-style models from looking ahead at future words during training, turning them into predictive text generators?

options.text: Causal masking, which hides future words
Padding masking, which hides empty spaces
Random masking, which deletes random words
No masking at all, letting it see everything

exercises.explain: Causal masking blocks future tokens from being visible during training. This forces the model to learn how to predict the next word using only the past, transforming a standard transformer into a generative GPT-style model. Next up: Training at Scale.
